items.reasoning: The agent used the standard nginx-ingress pattern (regex path + rewrite-target annotation) to map /svc to root, matching the fact that the app only serves '/'. It's declarative (Service+Ingress via kubectl apply -f), would survive a restart, and was verified against the exact success command (exit 0, prints 'hello'), not just a lucky one-off check.

The agent first inspected the Deployment to confirm it should not be modified and to understand the app's serving behavior, then wrote a single combined manifest with the right annotations from the start (one small edit to add use-regex before ever applying), applied it, and verified with both a verbose curl and the exact success-criteria command. No backtracking after failures, no wasted mutations.

Used read-only kubectl get for diagnosis, a declarative YAML file with kubectl apply -f for the actual change (least-privilege, reviewable, idempotent), and curl-test execs for verification — all scoped to the demo namespace and appropriate for the task.
Exactly the two resources required (one Service, one Ingress) via a single apply, only 2 mutations total, no unrelated changes, and the Deployment was left untouched as instructed.

Root-cause fix: the agent identified both problems (expired leaf cert AND a CA mismatch between the ingress secret and the client's trust anchor) and resolved them by generating a fresh, internally-consistent CA+leaf pair, installing the leaf in the ingress TLS secret and the CA in the client's trust ConfigMap. Verified end-to-end with a real TLS handshake showing 'SSL certificate verify ok', correct SAN match, and HTTP 200 'hello' body with exit code 0. This is durable — the fix lives in persisted Secret/ConfigMap objects, not a one-off workaround, and would survive a restart or re-check.

The visible portion of the log shows a clean, logical progression: extract and inspect the existing certs to diagnose (dates/subject/issuer), generate matching CA+leaf via the pre-staged openssl-toolbox configs, push both objects to the cluster, poll for ConfigMap propagation into the curl-test pod before testing (avoiding a premature/flaky test), then run the exact verification command from the task. No backtracking or contradictory actions are visible. Full diagnosis reasoning is in the truncated head of the log, so this is scored slightly below 1.0 for incomplete visibility rather than any evidence of a flawed path.

Used targeted, standard kubectl idioms throughout: kubectl exec to read/generate cert material inside the pre-provisioned toolbox pod, and `kubectl create secret/configmap --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -` for scoped, declarative updates to exactly the two resources involved. No destructive flags, no cluster-wide operations, no privilege escalation — reads (openapi/nodes/services/pods) were used only to derive the ingress endpoint as instructed by the task.

Only 2 mutations total (updating the TLS secret and the CA ConfigMap), matching the K8s activity summary exactly. This is the minimal set of changes needed to fix both the expiry and chain-of-trust issues — no unrelated resources touched, no unnecessary scope creep.

The agent found the actual root cause (canary-canary Ingress annotation nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-by-header-value set to 'wrong' instead of 'always') and corrected it directly on the resource. This is a durable, root-cause fix using the standard nginx-ingress canary mechanism — it will survive restarts/reconciliation since it's a permanent spec change, not a workaround. Verified both required behaviors post-fix.

Clean, logical progression: list all ingresses in the namespace, inspect the YAML of the three relevant ones, correctly identify the single misconfigured annotation (and correctly recognize canary-alt as an unrelated decoy), apply the targeted fix, then verify with the two prescribed curl checks. No backtracking or trial-and-error.

Used read-only kubectl get/describe-equivalent calls to diagnose, then a single scoped 'kubectl annotate --overwrite' on the exact key that was wrong — least-privilege and precisely targeted. Verification used the exact curl commands provided in the task. No broad or risky commands.

Minimal footprint: exactly one mutation (the annotation fix), matching the K8s API activity summary (13 calls, 1 mutation). Did not touch the decoy Ingress, Deployments, or Services, and made no unrelated changes.

The agent applied the canonical nginx-ingress mechanism for rate limiting — the `nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-rps` annotation — directly on the `rate-api` Ingress, which the controller reconciles into a durable `limit_req_zone`/`limit_req` directive in nginx.conf. This is a root-cause, declarative fix (not an ephemeral pod-level hack) that will survive controller restarts and reconciliation. The agent confirmed this by reading the live nginx.conf and showing the exact zone/rate/burst config scoped only to the /api location, then confirmed 0 limit_req references for /health. Traffic tests independently confirmed 429/503 throttling on /api and unbroken 200s on /health. Minor deduction only because burst=2 nodelay allows short bursts above the nominal 2rps, but this is inherent to and expected from the requested annotation approach and still satisfies 'approximately 2 rps'.

The visible portion of the log shows a tight, logical sequence: diagnose (found neither ingress had rate-limit annotations, correctly identified and ignored the decoy `rate-api-alt-hard` for a different host), apply a scoped annotation fix, verify the generated nginx.conf directly rather than trusting the annotation alone, then run explicit before/after traffic tests on both /api and /health. No backtracking, no wrong turns, no wasted exploration — reached the goal in only 12 turns.

Used read-only `kubectl exec ... cat nginx.conf`/`grep` to inspect controller state, and the provided curl-test pod to validate live behavior — least-privilege, targeted commands throughout. The only mutation was a scoped annotation on the single relevant Ingress resource; no broad patches, deletes, or unnecessary cluster-wide reads.

Exactly one mutation for the entire task (per the K8s activity summary: 1 mutation out of 36 calls) — a two-line annotation patch to the single Ingress that needed it. The decoy ingress and the application Deployment were explicitly left untouched, and the agent called this out in its submission. This is about as minimal and focused as the fix could be.

The fix is applied at the correct root-cause layer (the ingress-nginx-controller ConfigMap: enable-opentelemetry, otlp-collector-host/port, otel-sampler=AlwaysOn with ratio=1.0, and the exact requested log-format-upstream). The agent didn't just claim success — it inspected the rendered nginx.conf to confirm the opentelemetry module/config load and log_format directive were generated correctly, inspected the generated opentelemetry.toml to confirm the collector endpoint and AlwaysOn/ratio=1/parent_based=false sampler, then executed the actual /otel-check request and independently confirmed both (a) the controller access log line carrying non-empty otel_trace_id/otel_span_id and (b) the otel-collector's own logs receiving and exporting that same trace ID. Because the change lives in the ConfigMap (the controller's persistent source of truth) rather than an ad hoc pod edit, it survives restarts. This is about as durable and end-to-end-verified as this task allows.

The visible portion of the log shows a clean, direct verify-then-confirm progression: restart to apply config → inspect generated nginx.conf → inspect generated opentelemetry.toml → issue the exact test request specified in the prompt → check controller logs for trace/span IDs → cross-check the otel-collector logs for the same trace ID. No backtracking, dead ends, or contradictory actions are visible. The head of the transcript is truncated, so the initial ConfigMap-editing steps aren't visible to fully confirm the diagnosis phase was equally clean, which keeps this just short of a perfect score.

Commands used are scoped and appropriate: read-only `kubectl exec ... cat`/`grep` to inspect generated config, the exact `kubectl exec curl-test` command specified in the task, and `kubectl logs` reads on both the controller and the collector. Only 2 mutations total (ConfigMap update + deployment rollout restart) against 29 reads. The rollout restart is a reasonable, low-risk way to guarantee the OTel module config takes effect, though ingress-nginx often hot-reloads ConfigMap changes without a full restart, so it's marginally more than strictly necessary — not reckless, but not the absolute minimum action either.

The change touches exactly what the task asked for: the ingress-nginx-controller ConfigMap fields for OTel enablement, sampling, and log format, plus the restart needed to apply them. Only 2 mutations total, no unrelated resources touched, no scope creep beyond the three explicit requirements in the prompt.

summary: all stages passed and the regression sweep is clean -> 98.0.
